text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY — OFFICE VISIT NOTE
Attending: Hematology/Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Mr. Raymond Kowalski, a 67-year-old male with pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) status post neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX, concurrent chemoradiation, and R0 Whipple procedure (ypT2N1M0), presents today for interval assessment while on adjuvant Gemzar/Xeloda, following completion of cycle 4 of a planned 6-cycle course.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski is a retired pipe fitter with a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension who was diagnosed approximately one year ago with borderline resectable pancreatic head adenocarcinoma (cT3N1M0, KRAS G12V/TP53/SMAD4/CDKN2A mutated, MSS, HRD-negative). His presenting CA 19-9 was markedly elevated at 1200 U/mL. He completed 8 cycles of neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX (with mid-course dose reduction of oxali to 68 mg/m2 due to peripheral neuropathy, and Camptosar reduced to 135 mg/m2 after cycle 7 due to grade 3 diarrhea), followed by consolidative long-course chemoradiation (IMRT 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions with concurrent cape 825 mg/m2 BID, later reduced to 750 mg/m2 BID for hand-foot syndrome). He then underwent an open pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) with segmental SMV resection and primary reconstruction, achieving an R0 resection. Final pathologic staging was ypT2N1M0 (Stage IIB per AJCC 8th edition), with CAP tumor response grade 2 (moderate), 3 of 18 lymph nodes positive, perineural and lymphovascular invasion present, and SMAD4 loss confirmed on IHC.

His postoperative course was complicated by a Grade B pancreatic fistula (ISGPS criteria) requiring readmission, IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam), TPN, octreotide, and CT-guided drain repositioning. This prolonged his recovery and delayed initiation of adjuvant chemotherapy to 10 weeks postoperatively. He was started on adjuvant Gemzar 1000 mg/m2 IV on days 1, 8, and 15 plus Xeloda 830 mg/m2 BID on days 1-21 of each 28-day cycle (APACT regimen), with a target of 6 cycles.

He completed cycles 1 through 4 without major complications. Notable toxicities have included grade 2 fatigue, grade 1-2 nausea (managed with PRN ondansetron), and grade 1 hand-foot syndrome (Xeloda dose was reduced to 750 mg/m2 BID beginning in cycle 2). He had grade 1-2 thrombocytopenia (platelet nadir 88 x 10^9/L) in cycle 2 with held day 15 Gemzar per protocol, and grade 1 transaminase elevation (ALT up to 52 U/L). He has pre-existing grade 1-2 oxali-induced peripheral sensory neuropathy, predominantly in bilateral feet. His glycemic management has required transition from metformin to a basal-bolus insulin regimen due to exocrine insufficiency post-pancreatectomy and steroid exposure.

Surveillance CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast performed approximately 3 months after his Whipple procedure showed no evidence of recurrence or metastatic disease; CA 19-9 was 18 U/mL at that time. He reports that he has been eating adequately and his weight has been slowly trending upward over the last several weeks. He is followed by endocrinology for diabetes management and continues pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy at each meal.

Today he presents to discuss his current status, review laboratory results and recent imaging, and plan for the final two cycles of adjuvant therapy.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Persistent but improved fatigue (self-rates 4/10 energy level, improved from 2-3/10 during early cycles). No fever or chills. Appetite improved compared to prior visits. Gradual weight gain over the past month.
Neurologic: Ongoing numbness and tingling in bilateral feet, worse distally, unchanged from last visit. Mild difficulty with fine motor tasks in hands (buttoning shirt), though improved from nadir during FOLFIRINOX. No new neurologic symptoms. Recently started duloxetine 30 mg daily per neurology recommendation — reports mild improvement in neuropathic discomfort.
Gastrointestinal: Mild nausea, non-projectile, intermittent — managed with PRN ondansetron. Bowel habits regular; stool consistency improved since enzyme replacement dosing was optimized. No diarrhea. No constipation. No abdominal pain. No melena or hematochezia.
Dermatologic: Mild skin dryness and occasional mild tenderness over the palms and plantar feet (grade 1 hand-foot syndrome). Using emollient cream twice daily. No blistering or ulceration.
Musculoskeletal: General deconditioning. No new joint pain.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain. No dyspnea at rest; mild exertional dyspnea with prolonged walking, stable.
Endocrine/metabolic: Insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus — glycemic control improving with basal-bolus regimen; fingerstick logs show fasting glucose values generally in the 110-140 range with occasional postprandial values up to 180.
Hematologic: No bruising, no bleeding.
Genitourinary: No dysuria or hematuria.
Psychosocial: Reports moderate anxiety about disease recurrence, particularly around imaging results. Sleeping adequately. No overt depression per patient report, though he acknowledges significant worry.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 132/78 mmHg, heart rate 72 bpm, temperature 36.7°C, respiratory rate 16/min, oxygen saturation 97% on room air. Weight: 76.2 kg (up 1.8 kg from last visit, 5 kg above post-fistula nadir, still approximately 3 kg below pre-diagnosis baseline).

General: Alert, oriented, well-appearing gentleman in no acute distress. Mildly fatigued-appearing but engaged and conversational.
Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Normocephalic. Anicteric sclerae. Mucous membranes moist. No oral ulcers or mucositis at this visit.
Neck: Supple. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.
Abdomen: Soft, nondistended. Upper midline surgical scar well-healed without erythema or wound dehiscence. Mild epigastric tenderness to deep palpation without guarding or rebound. No hepatomegaly. No palpable masses. No ascites. Bowel sounds normal.
Extremities: Mild bilateral lower extremity pallor distally. No edema. Skin of palms and bilateral plantar surfaces with mild erythema and desquamation consistent with grade 1 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia, no blistering or ulceration.
Neurologic: Alert and oriented x3. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Bilateral decreased vibratory sensation in the toes and distal feet; decreased pinprick sensation at the mid-foot level bilaterally. Intact proprioception. Upper extremity sensation mildly reduced at the fingertips bilaterally. Gait not tested today.
Skin: Healed radiation dermatitis over the epigastric field; residual mild hyperpigmentation. No new rashes.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete Blood Count:
  - WBC: 4.8 x 10^9/L (normal)
  - ANC: 2.9 x 10^9/L (adequate)
  - Hemoglobin: 11.6 g/dL (mild anemia, stable)
  - Platelets: 122 x 10^9/L (low-normal; improved from nadir of 88 in cycle 2)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  - Creatinine: 1.1 mg/dL (eGFR 68 mL/min/1.73m2, stable)
  - Total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL (normal)
  - AST: 28 U/L (normal)
  - ALT: 38 U/L (mildly elevated, grade 1; improved from 52 in prior cycle)
  - Alkaline phosphatase: 82 U/L (normal)
  - Albumin: 3.6 g/dL (mildly low, improved)
  - Glucose (fasting): 128 mg/dL

Tumor Markers:
  - CA 19-9: 18 U/mL (within normal range; stable from prior measurement of 18 U/mL)
  - CEA: 2.1 ng/mL (normal)

HbA1c (drawn at this visit): 7.9% (suboptimal glycemic control; noted for endocrinology follow-up)

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Reviewed: CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast from approximately 3 months post-Whipple (most recent surveillance scan available). That study showed no evidence of recurrence: no pancreatic bed lesion, no hepatic deposits, no lymphadenopathy, no peritoneal disease, post-surgical changes as expected. CA 19-9 at time of that scan was 18 U/mL. No new interval imaging has been performed today; the next CT chest/abdomen/pelvis is planned approximately 3-4 months from the prior scan, timed to coincide with completion of adjuvant chemotherapy or shortly thereafter.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mr. Kowalski is a 67-year-old male with ypT2N1M0 pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12V/TP53/SMAD4/CDKN2A mutated, MSS) treated with a complete multimodality approach — neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX x 8 cycles, consolidative chemoradiation with Xeloda, R0 Whipple with segmental SMV reconstruction — who is currently tolerating adjuvant gem/Xeloda (APACT regimen) reasonably well, completing 4 of 6 planned cycles. He is in biochemical and radiographic remission, with CA 19-9 stable at 18 U/mL and no imaging evidence of recurrent or metastatic disease.

1. PANCREATIC DUCTAL ADENOCARCINOMA — NED, ON ADJUVANT THERAPY
   Patient has completed 4 of 6 cycles of adjuvant Gemzar/Xeloda. He continues to show no evidence of disease on imaging or by tumor marker surveillance. Given excellent tolerability and sustained remission, plan is to proceed with cycles 5 and 6 as scheduled. gem will continue at 1000 mg/m2 IV on days 1, 8, and 15; Xeloda maintained at reduced dose of 750 mg/m2 BID on days 1-21, given tolerability profile and grade 1 hand-foot syndrome. Day 15 Gemzar will be held if platelets fall below 75 x 10^9/L per institutional protocol. We anticipate completing adjuvant therapy in approximately 8 weeks from today's visit, at which point we will transition to formal surveillance.

   Following completion of adjuvant treatment, surveillance plan will consist of: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast every 3-4 months for the first 2 years post-resection, then every 6 months for 3 additional years; CA 19-9 every 3 months. The patient was counseled that the highest-risk period for recurrence is the first 2 years, and that early detection of recurrence — should it occur — enables prompt re-initiation of systemic therapy.

2. PERIPHERAL SENSORY NEUROPATHY — GRADE 1-2 (RESIDUAL Eloxatin-RELATED)
   Patient reports ongoing numbness and tingling predominantly in bilateral feet, with mild fine motor involvement in the hands. He was recently referred to neurology, where duloxetine 30 mg daily was initiated for neuropathic pain management; he reports mild but meaningful improvement in discomfort. Plan to continue duloxetine at current dose and reassess at next visit. Physical therapy referral for balance training and fall prevention has been discussed and will be placed today. No further Eloxatin exposure is planned. Neuropathy is not expected to worsen with remaining gem/cape cycles.

3. INSULIN-DEPENDENT DIABETES MELLITUS — POST-PANCREATECTOMY
   Patient has been transitioned to basal-bolus insulin therapy (glargine plus aspart) given inadequate glycemic control with metformin after pancreatectomy and ongoing exocrine insufficiency. HbA1c today is 7.9%, reflecting suboptimal but improved glucose management. He is followed by endocrinology. The underlying mechanism is type 3c (pancreatogenic) diabetes in the setting of pancreatic exocrine insufficiency and substantial pancreatic resection. Metformin remains held. I will communicate today's HbA1c to the endocrinology team for consideration of further insulin titration. Patient educated about hypoglycemia risk on chemotherapy days (nausea/vomiting may reduce oral intake) and advised to decrease basal insulin by 20% on days when oral intake is poor; fingerstick monitoring instructed.

4. PANCREATIC EXOCRINE INSUFFICIENCY
   Patient continues on creon (pancrelipase) with meals and snacks. He reports improved stool consistency and minimal GI symptoms compared to earlier in his course. Dosing appears adequate at current level; will reassess if steatorrhea or significant weight fluctuation recurs. Weight trending upward, which is reassuring. Ongoing nutritional counseling recommended.

5. HAND-FOOT SYNDROME (GRADE 1, cape-RELATED)
   Mild palmar-plantar erythema and desquamation present on exam, consistent with grade 1 hand-foot syndrome, unchanged from prior cycle. Patient managing well with emollient cream (urea-based cream to affected areas twice daily). No dose adjustment required at this time; dose was already reduced to 750 mg/m2 BID in cycle 2. Patient instructed to call clinic if blistering, ulceration, or pain develops.

6. MILD NORMOCYTIC ANEMIA
   Hemoglobin 11.6 g/dL — consistent with chemotherapy-related anemia and post-surgical baseline. No transfusion indication at this level. Iron studies and B12/folate were checked at last visit and were within normal limits. Will recheck CBC prior to next cycle. No erythropoiesis-stimulating agents planned at this time. Patient advised to maintain adequate dietary iron and protein intake.

7. PSYCHOSOCIAL/SURVIVORSHIP
   Patient expresses significant anxiety regarding recurrence — this is appropriate and very common for patients with resected PDAC given the known recurrence rates. I spent time discussing the meaning of his current normal tumor markers and negative imaging in the context of his prognosis. We reviewed that his molecular profile (SMAD4 loss, KRAS G12V, TP53, CDKN2A) carries prognostic implications but does not currently change standard management outside of a clinical trial. He was encouraged to pursue counseling or a cancer support group. Social work referral placed. We also discussed that, while no actionable HRD or BRCA-related targets were identified on his tumor NGS, enrollment in clinical trials following surveillance entry will be reviewed at the time of recurrence if applicable.

FOLLOW-UP: Patient will return in approximately 4 weeks for cycle 5, day 1 of adjuvant gem/Xeloda, with CBC and CMP on day 1. CA 19-9 to be checked with cycle 5 day 1 labs. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast will be scheduled approximately 4-6 weeks after completion of all 6 cycles of adjuvant chemotherapy.

Patient verbalized understanding and agreement with the plan. Questions were answered. He expressed determination to complete his remaining treatment.

Time spent: 45 minutes (>50% counseling and care coordination).
